replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you're doing a medieval-style talisman, quincunxes and semisquares don't matter; those are minor aspects, and in my experience their effect on a talismanic election is so small as to be undetectable. Beneficial aspects to malefics -- yeah, there's a lot of disagreement over that, and I suspect it has to do with the natal chart of the mage; if Mars is essentially or accidentally dignified in your natal chart, the Moon trine Mars will probably work to your benefit, while if you natal Mars is debilitated I'd stay away from it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As for your fourth question, asking what people did before TV, I'm old enough that I was about 10 when a few families in our neighborhood began to get the first small-screen black-and-white TVs.

In our family, between dinner and bedtime, we talked with one another, we read books (sometimes aloud to everyone), sometimes we played a few 78 rpm records, sometimes we played cards (Go Fish, and when we children were a bit older, Canasta) or board games (Sorry!, Parcheesi, Monopoly).

Dinner was ready to eat somewhere around 5 or a little later. Children were in bed by about 8:30; adults closed up the house and went to bed around 10 pm.

Mostly we kept on doing all those things after we got a TV, but now we would also watch one or two shows together a week. I remember "What's My Line?" and "The Ed Sullivan Show." My grandmother watched The Lawrence Whelk Show" once a week.

Before dinner (and after school, of course), there was "The Howdy Doody Show" for children, and "Pinhead and Foodini." (The latter was a puppet show: Foodini was an extremely muddle-headed and incompetent wizard, and Pinhead was his bungling assistant. Foodini's magic worked, but never resulted in anything even half-way close to what he wanted to do.

All this was well before anyone had ever heard of Elvis, much less of the Beatles.

IIRC, we got a color TV when I was already in High School, but I never got into the habit of watching it every evening. When I went off to college, I had neither a radio nor a TV in my room, so I spent most of my time reading. Sometimes there would be a small party on a weekend night. (I never roomed in a college dormitory, but always rented a small room in the city and took board--early morning breakfast only, served around 7:00 am--at a nearby house.)

It was truly a different world, and a very different college experience, in those far-off days.

comments.title: Gods in fiction
comments.content:
Hi john,

I am enjoying the Hali series and had a question about the deities therein. I have heard discussion about Lovecraft's gods being potent or viable for magical work and worship but never gave it much thought. While reading the third book in your series I was very interested to find a resemblance in Yhoundeh with something I had encountered in a peculiar astral state. It got me wondering about how exactly the gods reveal themselves and whether certain names and images are just gateways for our benefit. replies.title: Re: Gods in fiction
replies.content:
Okay, keep in mind that the only material in the old Cthulhu mythos stories about Yhoundeh is a few references in one Clark Ashton Smith story that identifies her as an elk goddess. The rest of it, I came up with myself -- and of course I drew heavily on actual mythologies, and also on my own astral experiences, in crafting my portrayal of Yhoundeh (and all the other Great Old Ones in the series.)

As for your broader question, the gods clothe themselves in images taken from our imaginations, because we literally can't even begin to comprehend them as they are. Thales -- the Greek guy who invented philosophy -- was famous for saying "All things are full of gods," and as far as I can tell, he was quite correct; if you approach the world with an openness to revelation (which is not the same thing as feeling entitled to revelation), you can begin to experience the divine presences in all things.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Why do aims or statements of intent such as "I am going to lose weight" fail so badly? I assumed that such statements mean nothing without a detailed strategy: Day 1: Eat no ice-cream, Day 2: Have a salad, etc. But such schemes also seem to have limited success, even completely rational ones. So I feel there is something more self-sabotaging going on here. Is it for example by aiming at "losing weight" you are almost setting up a mental hurdle for yourself which can not just stop your progress, but spring you back in the opposite direction you wanted to go?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, you've just opened one of the biggest cans of worms in contemporary American life. You may want to sit down and draw in a deep breath before we go on.

1) Diets make you gain weight. Scores of studies dating back to the 1950s have shown that when you go on a restricted-calorie diet, your body shifts gears to use calories more efficiently and store more of them as fat. Remember that your body doesn't know anything about current fashions in body shape; it's been conditioned by millions of years of experience to assume that when calories run short, that means there's a famine, and it ought to stash as many calories as possible so that you don't starve to death if things get worse. So when you go on a restricted-calorie diet, you've just guaranteed that you'll lose five pounds, level off, and then gain ten or more. What's more, the more often you do this, the more your body decides that famine must be a routine thing around here, and the more calories it stockpiles to keep you going.

2) Heredity is the single largest factor in how much you weigh. Health conditions unrelated to how much you eat are another large factor. Then there's the fact that food animals are pumped full of drugs to make them gain wait, and yet nobody's willing to talk about whether those same drugs make human beings gain weight. All this means that how much you eat and how much exercise you get have only a modest amount to do with how much you weigh.

3) The diet industry knows all this perfectly well. It's a multibillion dollar industry and it saturates the media with advertising (much of it not declared as such) to keep people on a self-defeating treadmill, trying to lose weight in ways that guarantee that they will gain weight. It's very lucrative -- and it explains why the more people in America obsess about losing weight, the more weight people gain...

So a different approach is clearly called for. Here's what I recommend.

First, do some serious journaling to figure out why you want to lose weight. Sort through the cultural conditioning; do some research on your own to find out where the dieting craze came from and who profits from it. Make your own decision about how to relate to your body, rather than letting a corrupt industry make that decision for you.

Second, eat a normal, healthy diet and get reasonable amounts of exercise. Let your body get used to the fact that it doesn't have to worry about starving, and keep it moving and fit without reference to weight.

Third, if you decide that you still want to lose weight, use affirmations without dieting, and remember the basic rule that an affirmation should always be in the present tense and express the thing you want to achieve, not the process of getting there. "I am going to lose weight" is an ineffective affirmation because it puts weight loss in the future. "I am thinner" would be better -- but it's much better still to focus on the goals you want to achieve via weight loss, such as "I am strong" or "I am beautiful." Remember that affirmations work by changing your self-talk and thus your self-image, and all other results follow from that; remember also that you can be thin and weak, or thin and ugly, or what have you, so aim for the thing you want to achieve and not for the step you think will get you there.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks for this.

replies.title: body weight
replies.content:
I'm 6'2" and 160 lbs, male, and have always believed "food is fuel and construction material; take only what you need, and leave the rest for others". If you focus on the consumption of nutrient-rich foods (meat, vegetables, whole grains), and the generosity of leaving much of it in the store for others to eat, you may eventually find that you're more satisfied with your weight, appearance, and health. You should also look with skepticism on MOST of what's on the shelves: "who wants me to buy this? Why do I care?"

Do not think "eating/drinking this will soothe my anxiety, deepen my friendships, and/or attract beautiful people to my side." Those are just the sales pitches.

Do not think "I can eat/drink this; there's no evidence that it will harm me..." Look for the evidence of what your body needs to make you healthy, and leave the ambiguous on the shelf.

Don't be afraid to put a little butter on your bread, or a strip of bacon in your omelet. You need eat only a little to celebrate how good it tastes.

Lathechuck

replies.title: Re: Geomancy questions
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Astrology
replies.content:
The word is "twilight."

In modern usage "twilight" is a rough synonym for "dusk," but it originally derives from the Old English word for "two," as in "two lights."
The same root can be found in "twin," "twist," "between," "twig" (a twig was originally a stick that forked into two), and not least in the modern English word "two."

My source is a book by Bryan Bunch titled "The Kingdom of Infinite Number: A Field Guide." It is a mathematics book, and the relevant information is in the entry for the number 2.

Of course words change, but I don't think current usage would allow you to use "twilight" in this sense without glossing it, and i further suspect that doing so would be an innovation rather than a retrogression.

First, I'm curious about platonic and neo-platonic philosophy from a druid standpoint: I believe I've read on a few occasions that you self-describe as someone very heavily influenced by that school of thought (and please forgive me if I misread). replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's a common misunderstanding of Platonism to see it as disparaging matter. Quite the contrary, matter is the final manifestation of the One, the thing that makes the cosmos complete. As Plato says in the Timaeus, the creator of the world made it as perfect as it could possibly be, given the limitations of manifest reality, and matter is part of that perfection. C.S. Lewis, who was the last of the red-hot Christian Platonists, used to respond to the disparagement of matter this way: "God likes matter. After all, He invented it."

2) All the sacraments are, among other things, magical workings that link your energy body to the holy beings of the Catholic tradition. You can renounce that and go on to establish relationships with other beings -- I know a lot of Druids who were raised Catholic and left that church for the usual reasons -- but it's a serious step. If you've already broken with the Catholic church, remember that other deities don't necessarily behave the way that Catholic sacred beings do; in particular, they're not watching you day and night to see if you masturbate. ;-)

comments.content: I have an issue with internet use. I use it much more than I want, and much more than I need to use it. Would an affirmation like, "I don't use the internet. I have no need of it," help?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, to begin with, one of the basic rules of affirmations is that you need to avoid negations such as "don't" and "no," because your subconscious doesn't process those. It will hear those affirmations as "I use the internet" and "I have need of it." (One of the reasons they're called affirmations is that they need to be put in affirmative terms.)

Second, any time you're dealing with a persistent habit, you need to understand where the habit comes from and what makes it attractive to you. Journaling, as described in the Magic Monday FAQ, is a good way to do this. Put some time into written dialogues with the internet, and with your own habit of internet use, and tease out the sources of your internet habit. They may go deeper than you expect.

Once you've done that, yes, an affirmation would be one way to go about changing things. Try something like this: breathing in, repeat silently, "The internet is boring." Breathing out, repeat silently, "I have a life instead." Then make a point of getting involved in other activities that keep you busy, away from the internet.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: From someone else who's had the same problem: it can be useful to figure out what you're getting from the internet and then find another way to get it. Are you lonely? Under-stimulated? Avoiding something else? For instance, I find I gravitate to the internet when I'm too tired to do anything more active, but not tired enough to sleep. My alternative is to read in bed, which satisfies the same conditions. In fact, I think I will now go take my own advice ;)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Joseph Smith Jr's own birth family was uncommonly deep into magical studies while he was growing up in Vermont. The earliest extant magician's black-handled knife made in the United States was made specifically for Joseph Smith Jr's father, and was etched with magical symbols out of Agrippa's "Three Books of Occult Philosophy" (probably by way of Francis Barrett's "The Magus"). Joseph himself built up quite a local reputation as a scryer in his younger years. Because of this I think it more likely than not that his golden tablets and his translation of them were something a little more complex than a simple con-job.

Myself, I would prefer simply to speak of his experiences in the "imaginal world" than on any specific plane of existence. (The "Imaginal World" is Henri Corbin's translation of an Arabic technical term. "Imaginal" lies half-way, more or less, between "real" and "imaginary." In my own view of magic, most effective magical work involves work in the imaginal world.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: For what it's worth, my take -- after several close readings of Corbin's paper Mundus Imaginalis -- is that what he calls the imaginal world and Sufi mystics call alam al-mithal, "the mystical world of similitudes where all corporeal limitations are removed," corresponds very closely to the astral plane as discussed in classic occult texts. Your mileage may vary, of course.
